message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier A) for a production-hardening pass on the hc-assay repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Determine whether this finding is a TRUE_POSITIVE (a genuine production-readiness defect that should be fixed) or a FALSE_POSITIVE (false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design, or not a real defect). Be rigorous and skeptical in BOTH directions — do not rubber-stamp, but do not dismiss a real defect on a technicality.

Out-of-scope-by-design exclusions (these are NOT defects): the dataset-agnostic engine receiving opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; transcripts intentionally committed (and scrubbed); on-box/loopback data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope limitations.

FINDING K-SEC-1 (medium): Local-URI guard rejects Unix-domain-socket Postgres host
Location: src/assay_engine/_local.py ~126-153 (require_local_uri) + is_loopback_host
Claim: require_local_uri() raises NonLocalEndpointError for a libpq Unix-domain-socket host (leading "/", e.g. host=/var/run/postgresql, or postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql), because is_loopback_host("/var/run/postgresql") is False. A leading-"/" host is libpq standard for a local Unix socket — strictly more data-sovereign than TCP loopback (which IS accepted), so the guard refuses the one local form that never touches the network. Reachable: checkpoint.py routes operator ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL through require_local_uri. No test/doc declares the rejection intentional. VERIFY empirically and confirm UNC //server/share still fails closed.

Read the actual code (Read/Grep/Bash) and, where the claim is empirically testable, REPRODUCE it (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ... ; use "python -m pytest" not bare pytest). Judge ONLY K-SEC-1. Give your verdict with concrete evidence (what you ran/read, what you observed).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify finding K-SEC-1 by reading the actual code and reproducing the claim.

content.content:
1	"""Data-sovereignty helper: enforce that network endpoints are loopback-only (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Every engine component that takes a host/URL for an on-box service (local model runtime,
4	tracing collector, vector store, checkpoint database) routes it through here so a non-local
5	address fails loud rather than silently shipping data off the machine.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	import ipaddress
11	import re
12	from urllib.parse import parse_qs, urlparse
13	
14	# A libpq keyword/value token: ``key = value`` with optional whitespace around ``=`` (libpq
15	# permits it) and an optionally single/double-quoted value (which may contain spaces).
16	_DSN_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"(\w+)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
17	
18	
19	class NonLocalEndpointError(RuntimeError):
20	    """Raised when a configured endpoint is not loopback (would leave the machine)."""
21	
22	
23	def is_loopback_host(host: str | None) -> bool:
24	    """True only for genuine loopback hosts.
25	
26	    Parses the host as an IP and tests the loopback network (127.0.0.0/8, ::1), so a public
27	    name that merely *starts with* ``127.`` (e.g. ``127.0.0.1.evil.com``) is correctly
28	    rejected. The literal ``localhost`` is the only accepted non-IP host.
29	    """
30	    if not host:
31	        return False
32	    h = host.strip().lower()
33	    if h == "localhost":
34	        return True
35	    try:
36	        return ipaddress.ip_address(h.strip("[]")).is_loopback
37	    except ValueError:
38	        return False
39	
40	
41	def require_loopback_url(url: str, *, what: str) -> str:
42	    """Return ``url`` if its host is loopback; else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`."""
43	    host = urlparse(url).hostname
44	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
45	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
46	            f"{what} must be loopback-only (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got host {host!r} "
47	            f"from {url!r}"
48	        )
49	    return url
50	
51	
52	def require_loopback_host(host: str, *, what: str) -> str:
53	    """Return ``host`` if it is loopback; else raise. (``0.0.0.0`` binds all interfaces and
54	    is correctly rejected — it would expose data off-box.)"""
55	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
56	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
57	            f"{what} must be a loopback host (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got {host!r}"
58	        )
59	    return host
60	
61	
62	def _authority_hosts(netloc: str) -> list[str]:
63	    """Every host in a URI authority, supporting libpq's comma-separated multi-host form.
64	
65	    ``urlparse().hostname`` returns only the *first* host of a multi-host authority
66	    (``host1:port1,host2:port2``), so a remote second host would slip past a single-host
67	    check (audit #D3). Each host may carry its own ``:port`` and IPv6 hosts are bracketed.
68	
69	    Userinfo is stripped at the **first** ``@`` — matching libpq, which (unlike ``urlparse``,
70	    which uses the last ``@``) treats the first ``@`` as the userinfo delimiter. A string like
71	    ``[REDACTED]:5432@localhost`` is therefore read as host ``evil.com`` here, not
72	    ``localhost`` (audit #D5). Any host element still containing ``@`` after that is ambiguous
73	    and is returned verbatim so the caller's loopback check fails it closed.
74	    """
75	    if "@" in netloc:
76	        netloc = netloc.split("@", 1)[1]
77	    hosts: list[str] = []
78	    for part in netloc.split(","):
79	        p = part.strip()
80	        if not p:
81	            continue
82	        if p.startswith("["):  # [ipv6] or [ipv6]:port
83	            end = p.find("]")
84	            hosts.append(p[1:end] if end != -1 else p[1:])
85	        else:
86	            hosts.append(p.rsplit(":", 1)[0] if ":" in p else p)
87	    return hosts
88	
89	
90	def require_local_uri(uri: str, *, what: str) -> str:
91	    """Return ``uri`` if it is local (ADR-0003), else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`.
92	
93	    Three forms are recognized, validated against what the real client (libpq for postgres,
94	    urllib for http, sqlite for files) actually connects to — not merely what ``urlparse``
95	    reports, since the two can disagree:
96	
97	    - **URI with an authority** (``scheme://host…`` or scheme-relative ``//host…``): *every*
98	      host in the authority must be loopback (incl. each of a comma-separated multi-host
99	      authority, #D3), AND any ``host``/``hostaddr`` query parameter that libpq would use to
100	      override the authority host (#D2). ``hostaddr`` is checked independently of ``host``.
101	    - **libpq keyword/value DSN** (no authority but ``key=value`` tokens): ``host``/``hostaddr``
102	      must be loopback, tolerating libpq's whitespace/quoting around ``=`` (#P1, #D1).
103	    - **bare path** (no authority, no ``=``): a local file/dir — but a Windows UNC path
104	      (``\\\\server\\share``) is rejected as remote.
105	
106	    Inputs that cannot be parsed safely fail closed (rejected), never accepted.
107	    """
108	    try:
109	        parsed = urlparse(uri)
110	        netloc = parsed.netloc
111	        query = parsed.query
112	    except ValueError:
113	        # e.g. a malformed IPv6 authority — we cannot determine the host, so refuse.
114	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
115	            f"{what}: endpoint could not be parsed safely; rejecting (ADR-0003)"
116	        ) from None
117	
118	    if netloc:
119	        # A backslash never legitimately appears in a URI authority; a WHATWG-normalizing
120	        # client (e.g. some HTTP stacks) treats '\' as '/', so it could read a different host
121	        # than urlparse. Refuse rather than risk that differential (audit #D6, defense-in-depth).
122	        if "\\" in netloc:
123	            raise NonLocalEndpointError(
124	                f"{what}: backslash in URI authority is not permitted (ADR-0003); rejecting"
125	            )
126	        for h in _authority_hosts(netloc):
127	            # A residual '@' means the authority is ambiguous between this parser and libpq —
128	            # fail closed (audit #D5).
129	            if "@" in h or not is_loopback_host(h):
130	                raise NonLocalEndpointError(
131	                    f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); authority "
132	                    f"names non-loopback host {h!r}"
133	                )
134	        for key in ("host", "hostaddr"):
135	            for val in parse_qs(query).get(key, []):
136	                h = val.strip()
137	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h):
138	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
139	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); URI query "
140	                        f"{key}={h!r} points off-box"
141	                    )
142	        return uri
143	
144	    if "=" in uri:  # libpq keyword/value DSN (no URI authority)
145	        for match in _DSN_TOKEN_RE.finditer(uri):
146	            if match.group(1).strip().lower() in {"host", "hostaddr"}:
147	                h = match.group(2).strip().strip("'\"")
148	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h):
149	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
150	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN names "
151	                        f"non-loopback host {h!r}"
152	                    )
153	        return uri
154	
155	    stripped = uri.lstrip()
156	    # Windows treats ANY two leading separators (in any mix of '\' and '/') as a UNC prefix
157	    # to a remote SMB share — \\srv, //srv, /\srv, \/srv all resolve off-box (audit #D6).
158	    # (The pure '//host' form is already caught by the authority branch above; covering it
159	    # here too keeps the branch self-contained.)
160	    if len(stripped) >= 2 and stripped[0] in "\\/" and stripped[1] in "\\/":
161	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
162	            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); UNC-style path "
163	            f"{uri!r} is a remote share"
164	        )
165	    return uri  # bare path / dir store
166

155	
156	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
157	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
158	
159	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
160	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
161	    """
162	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
163	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
164	
165	
166	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
167	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
168	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
169	    that can raise)."""
170	    try:
171	        pool.close()
172	    except Exception:
173	        pass
174

content.text: Reachability confirmed. Now reproduce the claim empirically.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri, is_loopback_host, NonLocalEndpointError

cases = [
  'host=/var/run/postgresql dbname=mydb',
  'postgresql:///mydb?host=/var/run/postgresql',
  'postgresql://localhost/db',
  'host=localhost dbname=db',
  '//server/share',
  r'\\\\server\\share',
]
for c in cases:
    try:
        r = require_local_uri(c, what='checkpointer connection')
        print('ACCEPT:', repr(c))
    except NonLocalEndpointError as e:
        print('REJECT:', repr(c), '->', str(e)[:80])

print('is_loopback_host(/var/run/postgresql) =', is_loopback_host('/var/run/postgresql'))
"
input.description: Reproduce Unix-socket host rejection and UNC fail-closed

content.content:
REJECT: 'host=/var/run/postgresql dbname=mydb' -> checkpointer connection must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN n
REJECT: 'postgresql:///mydb?host=/var/run/postgresql' -> checkpointer connection must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN n
ACCEPT: 'postgresql://localhost/db'
ACCEPT: 'host=localhost dbname=db'
REJECT: '//server/share' -> checkpointer connection must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); autho
REJECT: '\\\\server\\share' -> checkpointer connection must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); UNC-s
is_loopback_host(/var/run/postgresql) = False

content.text: Both claimed forms are rejected. Note the second case `postgresql:///mydb?host=/var/run/postgresql` has no netloc (empty authority), so it falls to the DSN branch (`=` present in the query), and the `host=/var/run/postgresql` token gets matched and rejected. Let me verify there's no test/doc declaring this rejection intentional.

content.text: No test or doc declares the Unix-socket rejection intentional. The tests cover loopback enforcement and UNC fail-closed, but nothing addresses the legitimate libpq Unix-domain-socket form. Let me confirm the bare-path local-file case is accepted to show the asymmetry the finding describes.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri
# bare local path accepted (single leading slash, no '=')
print('bare /var/run/postgresql ->', require_local_uri('/var/run/postgresql', what='x'))
# but as a libpq DSN host token it is rejected
"
input.description: Confirm bare local path accepted vs DSN host rejected

content.text: I have enough evidence to render a verdict.

input.reasoning:
Verified empirically against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py (require_local_uri, is_loopback_host) and confirmed reachability via src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:162-163 (get_postgres_connection_string routes the operator-supplied ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL through require_local_uri).

Reproduction (in .venv):
- DSN form `host=/var/run/postgresql dbname=mydb` -> REJECTED with NonLocalEndpointError ("DSN names non-loopback host"). The DSN branch (_local.py:144-153) matches the `host` token and tests is_loopback_host("/var/run/postgresql"), which returns False.
- URI form `postgresql:///mydb?host=/var/run/postgresql` -> REJECTED. It has an empty authority (no netloc), so it falls to the DSN branch via the `=` in the query string; the `host=/var/run/postgresql` token is matched and rejected the same way.
- is_loopback_host('/var/run/postgresql') == False (confirmed): a leading-"/" socket directory is neither "localhost" nor a parseable IP, so it fails the loopback test.

This is the libpq-standard form for a Unix-domain socket — a connection that never touches the network and is strictly more data-sovereign than TCP loopback. Yet:
- TCP loopback IS accepted (`postgresql://localhost/db`, `host=localhost`).
- A bare local path IS accepted: require_local_uri('/var/run/postgresql') returns the path unchanged (no `=`, single leading slash, so it hits the bare-path branch at :165). So the same string is accepted as a path but rejected as a libpq host token — an inconsistency that blocks the most local connection method.

Fail-closed behavior remains correct: `//server/share` -> REJECTED (authority branch), and `\\server\share` -> REJECTED (UNC branch at :160-164). So a fix to allow leading-"/" socket hosts must be scoped narrowly (only host/hostaddr tokens beginning with "/", not the `//`/UNC authority forms) to avoid weakening the UNC guard.

No test or doc declares the rejection intentional: grep of tests/, docs/, and ADR-0003 found no mention of Unix sockets; tests/test_persistence.py covers loopback enforcement, query-host override, DSN quoting/whitespace, multi-host, and UNC fail-closed, but never the legitimate socket form. ADR-0003 contains no socket/path carve-out either.

Not covered by any out-of-scope exclusion: on-box data sovereignty is the guard's own stated purpose (ADR-0003), and a Unix socket is the most on-box option; rejecting it is a genuine defect (a usability/correctness gap that forces operators away from the most sovereign connection path), not a deliberate design choice. Medium severity is appropriate: it does not weaken security (it is over-strict, failing safe) but it breaks a standard, recommended local Postgres configuration.

content.text: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The local-URI guard rejects the libpq Unix-domain-socket host form (`host=/var/run/postgresql` and `postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql`) while accepting TCP loopback and bare local paths — an over-strict inconsistency that blocks the most data-sovereign connection method, with no test or doc declaring it intentional. UNC fail-closed behavior is intact, so any fix must be narrowly scoped to host/hostaddr tokens with a leading "/".
